# PART I

## Item 1. Business

### Overview

Echoblast, Inc. ("Echoblast," "we," "us," "our," or the "Company") is an American cloud computing company specializing in GPU rental and managed artificial intelligence workloads. We were incorporated in Delaware in January 2025 and commenced operations in the first quarter of 2025. We are headquartered in San Francisco, California.

We operate a distributed GPU cloud platform that provides enterprises, research institutions, and artificial intelligence developers with on-demand access to high-performance computing infrastructure and managed services for training, inference, and evaluation of large language models and other machine learning workloads. Our mission is to democratize access to GPU compute resources and accelerate the development and deployment of artificial intelligence applications globally.

During fiscal year 2030, we generated total revenue of $522.9 million, representing significant growth from our annualized revenue run-rate of $295 million at the end of fiscal year 2029. At December 31, 2030, our annualized revenue run-rate was approximately $750 million, reflecting the accelerating demand for GPU infrastructure and our expanding customer base.

### Our Products and Services

We offer a comprehensive suite of products and services designed to simplify GPU access and AI workload management:

**Echoblast Cloud**

Echoblast Cloud is our foundational platform providing on-demand access to GPU compute resources. Customers can provision and manage GPU instances via our web console, API, or command-line interface. The platform supports various GPU architectures and configurations, enabling customers to select resources optimized for their specific workloads. Echoblast Cloud offers flexible billing models including on-demand, reserved capacity, and spot pricing to accommodate diverse customer requirements and cost optimization strategies.

**Echoblast Train**

Echoblast Train is our managed service for distributed model training. The service abstracts underlying infrastructure complexity by providing integrated support for popular machine learning frameworks, distributed training orchestration, multi-GPU and multi-node synchronization, automatic fault tolerance and checkpointing, and integrated monitoring and logging. Echoblast Train enables customers to focus on model development rather than infrastructure management, reducing time-to-market for AI applications.

**Echoblast Inference**

Echoblast Inference is our managed service for deploying and scaling machine learning models in production. The service provides auto-scaling capabilities, load balancing, model versioning, A/B testing infrastructure, and integrated observability. Echoblast Inference is designed to handle variable workload patterns while maintaining low latency and high throughput requirements common in production AI deployments.

**Echoblast Eval**

Echoblast Eval is our evaluation and benchmarking service that enables customers to systematically evaluate model performance across diverse datasets, metrics, and configurations. The service provides standardized evaluation infrastructure, comparative benchmarking capabilities, and integration with popular evaluation frameworks and datasets.

**Echoblast Marketplace**

Echoblast Marketplace is a curated platform connecting customers with pre-trained models, datasets, and third-party tools and services. Marketplace participants can list and monetize their offerings, creating an ecosystem that extends the value of our core platform.

### Our Technology and Infrastructure

Our platform is built on a distributed architecture designed for reliability, scalability, and performance. We employ a multi-region deployment strategy across North America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific regions to minimize latency and provide geographic redundancy. Our infrastructure management systems automatically provision, monitor, and maintain GPU resources across our fleet.

We have developed proprietary software for workload scheduling, resource allocation, fault detection and recovery, and performance optimization. Our systems are engineered to achieve high GPU utilization rates while maintaining responsive performance for customer workloads. We maintain integration with widely-adopted machine learning frameworks and tools, ensuring compatibility with existing customer workflows.

### Data Center Operations

As of December 31, 2030, we owned and operated a fleet of approximately 75,000 GPUs across multiple data centers. We employ a hybrid model combining owned infrastructure with strategic relationships with colocation providers and infrastructure partners. Our owned fleet consists primarily of NVIDIA H100, A100, and L40S GPUs, with selective deployment of alternative architectures to support diverse workload requirements.

We manage data center operations through a dedicated infrastructure team responsible for hardware procurement, deployment, monitoring, maintenance, and decommissioning. We maintain redundant cooling, power, and networking systems to ensure high availability and business continuity. Our data center operations are subject to industry-standard security protocols, environmental controls, and compliance requirements.

### Customers

Our customer base spans enterprises, research institutions, startups, and individual developers across multiple industries including technology, finance, healthcare, automotive, and media. We serve customers ranging from Fortune 500 companies to early-stage AI startups. Our largest customers are primarily large technology companies and well-funded AI research organizations.

During fiscal year 2030, our top ten customers represented approximately 42% of total revenue. While we have achieved significant customer diversification compared to prior periods, we remain dependent on a limited number of large customers for a material portion of our revenue. Customer acquisition and retention are critical to our growth strategy.

### Competition

The GPU cloud infrastructure market is competitive and includes both established cloud providers and specialized GPU infrastructure companies. Our principal competitors include CoreWeave, Lambda Labs, Nebius, and major hyperscale cloud providers including Amazon Web Services (AWS), Google Cloud Platform (GCP), and Microsoft Azure, which have begun expanding their GPU offerings.

Established cloud providers benefit from scale, brand recognition, existing customer relationships, and diversified service portfolios. Specialized competitors may offer differentiated services, superior price performance, or specialized support for specific workload types. We compete on the basis of performance, reliability, ease of use, pricing, customer support, and specialized services for AI workloads.

### Intellectual Property

We rely on a combination of patents, trade secrets, copyrights, and trademarks to protect our intellectual property. As of December 31, 2030, we held or had applications pending for multiple patents related to GPU resource scheduling, workload optimization, distributed training coordination, and fault tolerance mechanisms. We maintain confidentiality agreements with employees and contractors to protect trade secrets and proprietary technology.

We license certain third-party software under open-source and commercial licenses. We believe our use of third-party software complies with applicable license terms, though we cannot guarantee that third parties will not assert infringement claims against us.

### Human Capital

As of December 31, 2030, we employed approximately 500 full-time employees across engineering, operations, sales, customer success, and administrative functions. Our workforce is distributed across multiple geographic locations with significant concentrations in San Francisco, California and other technology hubs.

We invest in employee development through training programs, technical certifications, and career development opportunities. We offer competitive compensation packages including base salary, equity awards, health insurance, retirement benefits, and other benefits designed to attract and retain talented personnel.

Our human capital strategy emphasizes building a diverse and inclusive workforce. We maintain policies and programs designed to promote equal employment opportunity and prevent discrimination based on protected characteristics. We regularly assess workplace culture and employee satisfaction through surveys and feedback mechanisms.

### Government Regulation

Our operations are subject to various federal, state, and international laws and regulations. We comply with data protection regulations including the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) in the European Union, the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), and similar privacy laws in other jurisdictions. We maintain privacy policies and data handling procedures designed to comply with applicable requirements.

We are subject to export control regulations, including the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) and International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR), which restrict the export of certain technologies and technical data. We maintain compliance programs to ensure adherence to export control requirements, particularly regarding GPU access and AI technology.

We comply with environmental regulations applicable to data center operations, including energy efficiency standards and electronic waste disposal requirements. We maintain environmental compliance programs and monitor regulatory developments in this area.

---

## Item 1A. Risk Factors

Investors should carefully consider the following risk factors and other information contained in this Annual Report on Form 10-K.

### Risks Related to Our Business and Operations

**We depend on a limited number of large customers for a material portion of our revenue, and the loss of any significant customer could materially harm our business.**

During fiscal year 2030, our top ten customers represented approximately 42% of total revenue. We do not have long-term contracts with many of our customers, and they may terminate or reduce their usage of our services at any time. The loss of one or more significant customers, material reductions in their usage, or failure to renew contracts could materially reduce our revenue and harm our financial condition.

**We depend heavily on NVIDIA GPU supply and pricing, and supply constraints or price fluctuations could materially impact our business.**

Our operations depend on the availability of NVIDIA GPUs, which represent the substantial majority of our fleet. We have no guaranteed supply arrangements with NVIDIA and depend on our ability to purchase GPUs through standard channels at competitive prices. Supply constraints, allocation restrictions, or significant price increases could limit our ability to expand our fleet, increase our cost of goods sold, and reduce our competitiveness. Geopolitical tensions, trade restrictions, or other factors affecting NVIDIA's supply chain could exacerbate these risks.

**Competition from established cloud providers and specialized competitors could reduce our market share and pricing power.**

The GPU cloud infrastructure market is increasingly competitive. Established cloud providers including AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure have significantly expanded their GPU offerings and can leverage existing customer relationships, scale, and financial resources. Specialized competitors such as CoreWeave and Lambda Labs may offer superior pricing, performance, or specialized services. Intensifying competition could pressure our pricing, reduce customer acquisition rates, and require increased investment in product development and marketing.

**We face risks related to data center operations and infrastructure availability.**

Our operations depend on the reliable operation of data centers across multiple geographic regions. Data center outages, equipment failures, natural disasters, or other disruptions could interrupt customer service, result in data loss, and damage our reputation. While we maintain redundancy and backup systems, we cannot guarantee continuous availability. Significant outages could result in customer churn, revenue loss, and liability claims.

**Our data center leases and colocation arrangements expose us to operational and financial risks.**

We operate a hybrid model combining owned infrastructure with leased data center capacity and colocation arrangements. Lease terminations, price increases, or service disruptions by landlords or colocation providers could force us to relocate equipment, incur substantial costs, or lose capacity. Long-term lease commitments also create fixed cost obligations that could constrain financial flexibility if revenue declines.

**We face intense pressure to maintain and improve our pricing and unit economics.**

Customers increasingly expect competitive pricing and favorable terms. Pressure to reduce prices could reduce our gross margin and profitability. We must continuously optimize our cost structure and improve operational efficiency to maintain healthy unit economics and competitive pricing while growing revenue.

**Rapid technological change could render our products and services obsolete.**

The AI and GPU infrastructure markets are characterized by rapid technological advancement. Emergence of new GPU architectures, alternative compute technologies, or competing service models could reduce demand for our offerings. We must continuously invest in research and development to maintain technological competitiveness.

### Risks Related to Our Industry and Markets

**Demand for GPU infrastructure and AI services is uncertain and could decline.**

Our business depends on sustained demand for GPU compute resources and AI services. If demand for AI applications slows or plateaus, or if customers reduce their GPU spending, our revenue and growth could be materially impacted. Economic downturns, shifts in technology adoption patterns, or regulatory restrictions on AI development could reduce demand.

**Regulatory restrictions on AI development could materially harm our business.**

Governments worldwide are considering or implementing regulations governing AI development, deployment, and use. Restrictions on AI capabilities, requirements for model evaluation or licensing, export controls on AI technology, or other regulatory measures could reduce demand for our services or increase our compliance costs. We cannot predict the scope or impact of future AI regulations.

**We face evolving data privacy and security regulations that could increase compliance costs and expose us to liability.**

We are subject to data protection regulations including GDPR, CCPA, and similar laws in other jurisdictions. Compliance with these regulations requires investment in privacy controls, data handling procedures, and employee training. Regulatory changes could increase compliance costs. Privacy breaches or non-compliance could result in regulatory penalties, customer claims, and reputational harm.

**Export controls and trade restrictions could limit our ability to serve international customers and expand globally.**

Export control regulations restrict the export of GPUs and AI technology to certain countries and end-users. Trade tensions and geopolitical conflicts could result in additional restrictions. These limitations could reduce our addressable market and international growth opportunities.

### Risks Related to Our Financial Condition and Liquidity

**We have limited operating history, and our future profitability is uncertain.**

We were incorporated in January 2025 and commenced operations in the first quarter of 2025. We have limited operating history from which to evaluate our business model and future prospects. We cannot assure that we will achieve or maintain profitability. Our operating margins could be negatively impacted by increases in infrastructure costs, pricing pressure, or operational inefficiencies.

**We have significant capital requirements, and our ability to fund growth depends on capital availability.**

Our business requires substantial capital investment in GPU hardware, data center infrastructure, and software development. We cannot assure that we will have sufficient capital or access to capital markets to fund our growth plans. Inability to raise capital could limit our expansion and competitive position.

**We may not be able to maintain or improve our gross margins.**

Our gross margin of 33.4% in fiscal year 2030 is subject to various pressures including GPU costs, data center operating expenses, and customer mix changes. Inability to maintain or improve gross margins could reduce profitability and cash generation.

### Risks Related to Cybersecurity and Data Protection

**Cybersecurity breaches or attacks could expose customer data, disrupt our operations, and harm our reputation.**

We store and process sensitive customer data and proprietary information. Cybersecurity attacks including hacking, malware, ransomware, or other threats could result in unauthorized access to data, service disruptions, or data loss. Significant security incidents could result in customer churn, regulatory penalties, and litigation. We cannot guarantee that our security measures will prevent all attacks.

**We depend on third-party infrastructure and service providers for critical functions, creating cybersecurity and operational risks.**

We depend on third-party providers for colocation services, networking, and other critical infrastructure. Security breaches or service disruptions by these providers could impact our operations and customer service. We have limited control over third-party security practices.

---

## Item 1B. Unresolved Staff Comments

None.

---

## Item 1C. Cybersecurity

We recognize that cybersecurity is critical to protecting our business, customer data, and stakeholder interests. We maintain a comprehensive cybersecurity program designed to identify, protect against, detect, respond to, and recover from cybersecurity threats.

**Cybersecurity Governance and Oversight**

Our Board of Directors and Audit Committee provide oversight of cybersecurity risk management. The Board receives periodic reports on cybersecurity threats, incidents, and mitigation measures. Our Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) reports to the Chief Technology Officer and maintains direct communication channels with senior management and the Board regarding significant cybersecurity matters.

**Cybersecurity Risk Management**

Our cybersecurity program includes:

- Risk assessments and vulnerability scanning to identify security gaps and threats
- Access controls and identity management to restrict unauthorized access
- Encryption of data in transit and at rest
- Network segmentation and intrusion detection systems
- Security monitoring and incident response procedures
- Employee security awareness training and phishing simulations
- Third-party security assessments and vendor risk management
- Incident response and business continuity planning

**Security Incidents**

We maintain procedures for identifying, investigating, and responding to cybersecurity incidents. During fiscal year 2030, we did not experience any material security breaches or incidents that materially affected our operations or customer data. We maintain cyber liability insurance to mitigate financial impacts of potential security incidents.

**Third-Party Risk Management**

We conduct security assessments of critical third-party service providers including colocation providers and cloud service providers. We maintain contractual requirements for security standards and incident notification procedures.

---

## Item 2. Properties

**Principal Facilities**

Our principal executive offices are located at 500 Sansome Street, San Francisco, California 94111, which we lease under a multi-year lease agreement. This facility houses our corporate headquarters, including executive offices, engineering teams, sales, and customer success functions.

**Data Center Facilities**

We operate or have access to GPU compute resources across multiple data centers in the following regions:

- **North America**: Primary data centers in Northern California, Northern Virginia, and Toronto, Canada
- **Europe**: Data centers in Frankfurt, Germany and London, United Kingdom
- **Asia-Pacific**: Data centers in Tokyo, Japan and Singapore

Our data center strategy combines owned infrastructure with colocation arrangements. As of December 31, 2030, we owned approximately 75,000 GPUs deployed across these facilities. We maintain service level agreements with colocation providers specifying uptime requirements, support levels, and termination provisions.

**Lease and Occupancy Obligations**

Our lease obligations for office and data center space total approximately $150 million over the remaining lease terms. These commitments reflect our expansion of data center capacity to support growing customer demand. Lease obligations are recognized as operating lease liabilities under ASC 842.

We believe our current facilities are adequate to support operations and growth plans for the foreseeable future. We continue to evaluate opportunities to optimize data center footprint and expand capacity in strategic locations.

---

## Item 3. Legal Proceedings

We are not aware of any material pending or threatened litigation, regulatory investigations, or other legal proceedings that could materially impact our business, financial condition, or operations. In the ordinary course of business, we may be subject to routine customer disputes, employment claims, or other matters typical of our industry. We do not believe any such matters will have a material adverse effect on our business.

---

## Item 4. Mine Safety Disclosures

Not applicable. We are not engaged in mining operations.

---

# PART II

## Item 5. Market for Registrant's Common Equity, Related Stockholder Matters and Issuer Purchases of Equity Securities

### Market Information

Our Class A Common Stock is listed on The Nasdaq Global Select Market under the ticker symbol "EBLA". We completed our initial public offering in the first quarter of 2030 at an initial offering price of $18.00 per share.

### Stock Performance

The following table presents the high and low closing prices of our Class A Common Stock for each quarter of fiscal year 2030:

| Quarter | High | Low | Close |
|---------|------|-----|-------|
| Q1 2030 | $28.50 | $16.75 | $24.10 |
| Q2 2030 | $31.20 | $22.80 | $29.35 |
| Q3 2030 | $35.80 | $27.50 | $33.10 |

As of February 14, 2031, the closing price of our Class A Common Stock was $41.25 per share, and there were approximately 287,500,000 shares outstanding.

### Dividend Policy

We have not declared or paid cash dividends on our common stock and do not anticipate paying dividends in the foreseeable future. We expect to retain earnings to fund operations, capital investments, and growth initiatives.

### Issuer Purchases of Equity Securities

We did not repurchase any shares of our common stock during fiscal year 2030. We have not adopted a share repurchase program.

## Item 6. [Reserved]

---

## Item 7. Management's Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations

The following discussion and analysis should be read in conjunction with our consolidated financial statements and related notes included in Item 8 of this Annual Report on Form 10-K.

### Overview

Echoblast, Inc. is a GPU cloud infrastructure and managed AI services provider. We generate revenue primarily through the provision of on-demand GPU compute resources and managed services for model training, inference, evaluation, and related AI workloads.

During fiscal year 2030, we achieved significant growth milestones:

- Total revenue of $522.9 million, representing growth of 77% compared to fiscal year 2029
- Annualized revenue run-rate of $750 million at December 31, 2030, compared to $295 million at December 31, 2029
- GPU fleet expansion to approximately 75,000 owned GPUs
- Expansion of our customer base with improved diversification
- Improvement in operating leverage with operating margin of 17.1%

### Key Business Metrics

We monitor the following key business metrics to evaluate our business performance and growth:

**Annualized Revenue Run-Rate (ARR)**

ARR represents our revenue run-rate at period end, calculated by annualizing the revenue from the final month of the period. ARR is a non-GAAP measure that we believe provides useful information about our business trajectory and customer demand.

| Metric | FY 2030 | FY 2029 | Growth |
|--------|---------|---------|--------|
| ARR ($ millions) | $750.0 | $295.0 | 154% |

**GPU Fleet Utilization**

We track average GPU utilization rates as a percentage of total available capacity. Higher utilization rates generally indicate more efficient use of our infrastructure and stronger customer demand.

| Metric | FY 2030 | FY 2029 |
|--------|---------|---------|
| Average GPU Utilization | 68% | 52% |

**Net Revenue Retention**

Net Revenue Retention (NRR) measures the percentage of prior-year revenue retained from existing customers, including expansion revenue from increased usage and accounting for customer churn. An NRR above 100% indicates expansion revenue exceeds churn.

| Metric | FY 2030 | FY 2029 |
|--------|---------|---------|
| Net Revenue Retention | 142% | 118% |

### Components of Results of Operations

**Revenue**

We generate revenue from the following sources:

- **GPU Compute Services**: On-demand GPU rental and reserved capacity
- **Managed Services**: Training, inference, evaluation, and related services
- **Marketplace**: Commission revenue from third-party offerings
- **Professional Services**: Custom integration and consulting services

Revenue is recognized when performance obligations are satisfied, typically as compute resources are consumed or services are delivered.

**Cost of Revenue**

Cost of revenue consists primarily of:

- GPU hardware costs and depreciation
- Data center facility costs including rent, power, and cooling
- Network bandwidth and connectivity costs
- Personnel costs for infrastructure operations and support
- Third-party software licenses and support

**Gross Profit and Gross Margin**

Gross profit represents revenue minus cost of revenue. Gross margin is calculated as gross profit divided by revenue, expressed as a percentage.

**Operating Expenses**

Operating expenses consist of:

- **Research and Development**: Personnel and infrastructure costs for product development, engineering, and infrastructure innovation
- **Sales and Marketing**: Personnel, marketing programs, customer acquisition, and brand development
- **General and Administrative**: Personnel, facilities, legal, accounting, and other corporate functions

**Operating Income**

Operating income represents gross profit minus operating expenses.

### Results of Operations

**Fiscal Year 2030 Compared to Fiscal Year 2029**

| (in millions, except percentages) | FY 2030 | FY 2029 | Change | % Change |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Revenue | $522.9 | $295.0 | $227.9 | 77% |
| Cost of Revenue | $348.6 | $215.7 | $132.9 | 62% |
| Gross Profit | $174.3 | $79.3 | $95.0 | 120% |
| Gross Margin | 33.4% | 26.9% | 6.5 pp | — |
| Operating Expenses | $89.6 | $68.2 | $21.4 | 31% |
| Operating Income | $84.7 | $11.1 | $73.6 | 663% |
| Operating Margin | 17.1% | 3.8% | 13.3 pp | — |
| Net Income | $62.1 | $5.8 | $56.3 | 971% |
| Net Margin | 11.9% | 2.0% | 9.9 pp | — |

**Revenue**

Revenue increased 77% from $295.0 million in fiscal year 2029 to $522.9 million in fiscal year 2030. The increase was driven by:

- Expansion of our GPU fleet from approximately 45,000 GPUs at year-end 2029 to 75,000 GPUs at year-end 2030
- Increased customer adoption and usage of our platform
- Improved customer retention and net revenue retention rate of 142%
- Expansion of our managed services offerings including Train, Inference, and Eval
- Geographic expansion into European and Asia-Pacific markets

**Cost of Revenue and Gross Profit**

Cost of revenue increased 62% from $215.7 million in fiscal year 2029 to $348.6 million in fiscal year 2030, growing slower than revenue growth. Gross profit increased 120% from $79.3 million to $174.3 million, and gross margin improved from 26.9% to 33.4%, reflecting:

- Improved utilization of our GPU fleet from 52% to 68%
- Scale benefits in data center operations and infrastructure costs
- Better negotiation of hardware procurement costs as we increased volume
- Operational efficiency improvements in infrastructure management

**Operating Expenses**

Operating expenses increased 31% from $68.2 million in fiscal year 2029 to $89.6 million in fiscal year 2030, growing significantly slower than revenue. This deceleration in operating expense growth reflects improved operating leverage as we scale the business. Operating expenses as a percentage of revenue declined from 23.1% to 17.1%.

The increase in operating expenses was attributable to:

- Increased research and development investment in product features and platform capabilities
- Expansion of sales and customer success teams to support customer growth
- General and administrative expenses to support a larger organization

**Operating Income and Net Income**

Operating income increased substantially from $11.1 million (3.8% margin) in fiscal year 2029 to $84.7 million (17.1% margin) in fiscal year 2030. Net income increased from $5.8 million (2.0% margin) to $62.1 million (11.9% margin), reflecting the significant improvement in operating profitability and lower tax rates.

### Liquidity and Capital Resources

**Cash Position**

As of December 31, 2030, we had cash and cash equivalents of $285 million, compared to $198 million at December 31, 2029. The increase reflects strong operating cash flow generation and disciplined capital allocation.

**Operating Cash Flow**

For fiscal year 2030, operating cash flow was $95 million, compared to $32 million in fiscal year 2029. The improvement reflects increased profitability and better working capital management.

**Capital Expenditures**

We invested $142 million in capital expenditures during fiscal year 2030, primarily for GPU hardware procurement and data center infrastructure expansion. This represented a significant increase from $78 million in fiscal year 2029, reflecting our fleet expansion strategy.

**Liquidity Assessment**

We believe our current cash position, operating cash flow, and access to capital markets provide adequate liquidity to fund operations, capital investments, and growth initiatives for the foreseeable future. We do not have any material debt obligations and maintain a strong balance sheet.

### Critical Accounting Estimates

**Revenue Recognition**

We recognize revenue when performance obligations are satisfied. For GPU compute services, revenue is recognized as compute resources are consumed, typically on a daily or monthly basis. For managed services, revenue is recognized as services are delivered. Revenue is measured at the transaction price, which reflects the amount we expect to receive from customers.

**Depreciation of GPU Hardware**

We depreciate GPU hardware using the straight-line method over an estimated useful life of 4 years. Actual useful lives may differ from estimates, which could impact depreciation expense and asset values. We regularly assess useful life estimates based on technological advancement and market conditions.

**Allowance for Doubtful Accounts**

We maintain an allowance for doubtful accounts based on historical collection experience and assessment of customer creditworthiness. We write off accounts when collection is determined to be unlikely. Changes in customer creditworthiness or collection experience could affect the allowance.

**Deferred Revenue**

We receive advance payments from some customers for reserved capacity or prepaid usage. These amounts are recorded as deferred revenue and recognized as revenue as services are delivered or capacity is consumed.

---

## Item 7A. Quantitative and Qualitative Disclosures About Market Risk

### Interest Rate Risk

We have minimal exposure to interest rate risk. We maintain cash balances in interest-bearing deposit accounts and money market funds. Increases or decreases in interest rates would have minimal impact on our financial condition given the low interest rate environment.

### Foreign Exchange Risk

We generate revenue from international customers and incur expenses in foreign currencies, primarily in Euros and British Pounds. Fluctuations in foreign exchange rates could impact our reported revenue and expenses. We do not currently hedge foreign exchange exposure but monitor currency trends and may implement hedging strategies if exposure becomes material.

### Commodity Price Risk

Our business is exposed to GPU hardware prices, which are influenced by semiconductor market conditions and NVIDIA pricing. We do not have hedging instruments for GPU price risk. We manage this risk through diversification of suppliers and strategic procurement planning.

---

## Item 8. Financial Statements and Supplementary Data

### Report of Independent Registered Public Accounting Firm

To the Stockholders and Board of Directors of Echoblast, Inc.

**Opinion on the Financial Statements**

We have audited the accompanying consolidated balance sheets of Echoblast, Inc. as of December 31, 2030 and 2029, the related consolidated statements of operations, comprehensive income, stockholders' equity, and cash flows for the years then ended, and the related notes to the consolidated financial statements. In our opinion, the financial statements present fairly, in all material respects, the financial position of the Company as of December 31, 2030 and 2029, and the results of its operations and its cash flows for the years then ended, in conformity with U.S. generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP).

**Basis for Opinion**

These financial statements are the responsibility of the Company's management. Our responsibility is to express an opinion on the Company's financial statements based on our audits. We conducted our audits in accordance with the standards of the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB). Those standards require that we plan and perform the audit to obtain reasonable assurance about whether the financial statements are free of material misstatement, whether due to error or fraud.

An audit involves performing procedures to obtain audit evidence about the amounts and disclosures in the financial statements. The procedures selected depend on our judgment, including the assessment of the risks of material misstatement of the financial statements, whether due to error or fraud. In making those assessments, we examine, internal control over financial reporting. An audit also includes evaluating the appropriateness of accounting policies used and the reasonableness of significant accounting estimates made by management, as well as evaluating the overall presentation of the financial statements.

We believe that the audit evidence we have obtained is sufficient and appropriate to provide a basis for our audit opinion.

**Attestation Report on Internal Control Over Financial Reporting**

We have also audited, in accordance with the standards of the PCAOB, the Company's internal control over financial reporting as of December 31, 2030, based on criteria established in the framework in Internal Control—Integrated Framework issued by the Committee of Sponsoring Organizations of the Treadway Commission. In our opinion, the Company maintained, in all material respects, effective internal control over financial reporting as of December 31, 2030.

---

### Consolidated Balance Sheets

**ECHOBLAST, INC.**

### Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements

#### Note 1. Organization and Description of Business

Echoblast, Inc. ("Echoblast," "the Company," "we," "us," or "our") is a Delaware corporation incorporated in January 2025. We are a GPU cloud infrastructure and managed artificial intelligence services provider headquartered in San Francisco, California. We provide on-demand access to GPU compute resources and managed services for training, inference, and evaluation of machine learning models.

#### Note 2. Basis of Presentation and Accounting Policies

**Fiscal Year**

Our fiscal year ends on December 31.

**Principles of Consolidation**

The consolidated financial statements include the accounts of Echoblast, Inc. and our wholly-owned subsidiaries. All intercompany transactions and balances have been eliminated in consolidation.

**Use of Estimates**

The preparation of financial statements in accordance with U.S. GAAP requires management to make estimates and assumptions that affect the reported amounts of assets, liabilities, revenue, and expenses. Actual results could differ from these estimates.

**Revenue Recognition**

We recognize revenue in accordance with ASC 606 (Revenue from Contracts with Customers). Revenue is recognized when performance obligations are satisfied by transferring promised goods or services to customers at amounts that reflect the consideration we expect to receive.

For GPU compute services, revenue is recognized as compute resources are consumed, typically on a daily or monthly basis. For managed services including training, inference, and evaluation, revenue is recognized as services are delivered. For marketplace commission revenue, revenue is recognized when transactions occur.

**Cost of Revenue**

Cost of revenue consists of GPU hardware depreciation, data center facility costs, network bandwidth, personnel costs for infrastructure operations, and third-party software costs. These costs are expensed as incurred.

**Depreciation**

GPU hardware is depreciated using the straight-line method over an estimated useful life of 4 years. Other property and equipment is depreciated over useful lives ranging from 3 to 7 years. Leasehold improvements are amortized over the shorter of the lease term or the estimated useful life.

**Research and Development**

Research and development expenses consist of personnel costs, infrastructure costs, and third-party services related to product development and platform innovation. These costs are expensed as incurred.

**Sales and Marketing**

Sales and marketing expenses consist of personnel costs, marketing programs, customer acquisition costs, and brand development. These costs are expensed as incurred.

**General and Administrative**

General and administrative expenses consist of personnel costs for executive, finance, legal, and human resources functions, as well as facilities, insurance, and other corporate costs. These costs are expensed as incurred.

**Stock-Based Compensation**

We issue stock options and restricted stock units (RSUs) to employees and consultants. Stock-based compensation is measured at fair value using the Black-Scholes option pricing model for options and the stock price at grant date for RSUs. Compensation expense is recognized ratably over the vesting period, typically 4 years.

**Income Taxes**

We account for income taxes using the asset and liability method. Deferred income tax assets and liabilities are recognized for the expected future tax consequences of temporary differences between book and tax bases of assets and liabilities. We evaluate the realizability of deferred tax assets and establish valuation allowances when necessary.

**Cash and Cash Equivalents**

Cash and cash equivalents include cash on hand and highly liquid investments with maturities of three months or less.

**Accounts Receivable**

Accounts receivable are recorded at net realizable value. We maintain an allowance for doubtful accounts based on historical collection experience and assessment of customer creditworthiness. We write off accounts when collection is determined to be unlikely.

**Operating Leases**

We lease office and data center facilities under operating lease agreements. Right-of-use (ROU) assets and operating lease liabilities are recognized at lease commencement based on the present value of lease payments. ROU assets are amortized and lease liabilities are accreted over the lease term.

**Deferred Revenue**

We receive advance payments from customers for reserved capacity and prepaid usage. These amounts are recorded as deferred revenue and recognized as revenue as services are delivered or capacity is consumed.

**Business Segments**

We operate as a single business segment providing GPU infrastructure and managed AI services. We do not separately track revenue by geographic region for segment reporting purposes.

**GPU Hardware Commitments**

**Legal Matters**

## Item 9A. Controls and Procedures

### Evaluation of Disclosure Controls and Procedures

Our Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer, with assistance from our Disclosure Committee, evaluated the effectiveness of our disclosure controls and procedures (as defined in Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e) under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934) as of December 31, 2030. Based on this evaluation, our Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer concluded that our disclosure controls and procedures were effective as of December 31, 2030 to ensure that information required to be disclosed in our periodic SEC filings is recorded, processed, summarized, and reported within the time periods specified by SEC rules and regulations.

### Management's Assessment of Internal Control Over Financial Reporting

Our management is responsible for establishing and maintaining adequate internal control over financial reporting (as defined in Rule 13a-15(f) under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934). Our internal control over financial reporting is designed to provide reasonable assurance regarding the reliability of financial reporting and the preparation of financial statements for external purposes in accordance with GAAP.

Because of the inherent limitations of internal control over financial reporting, including the possibility of human error and the circumvention or overriding of controls, internal control over financial reporting may not prevent or detect all misstatements. Additionally, projections of any evaluation of effectiveness to future periods are subject to the risk that controls may become inadequate because of changes in conditions or that the degree of compliance with the policies or procedures may deteriorate.

Our management assessed the effectiveness of our internal control over financial reporting as of December 31, 2030 using the criteria established in the Internal Control—Integrated Framework issued by the Committee of Sponsoring Organizations of the Treadway Commission. Based on this assessment, management concluded that our internal control over financial reporting was effective as of December 31, 2030.

### Changes in Internal Control Over Financial Reporting

There were no changes in our internal control over financial reporting during the fourth quarter of fiscal year 2030 that materially affected, or are reasonably likely to materially affect, our internal control over financial reporting.

## Item 11. Executive Compensation

### Named Executive Officers

The following table sets forth compensation information for our named executive officers (NEOs) for fiscal year 2030:

| Name | Title | Salary | Bonus | Stock Awards | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Will Coleman | CEO | $400,000 | $150,000 | $2,100,000 | $2,650,000 |
| Luis Tran | CFO | $320,000 | $100,000 | $1,200,000 | $1,620,000 |
| Mark Howell | CTO | $380,000 | $120,000 | $1,800,000 | $2,300,000 |
| Angela Holmes | President | $350,000 | $110,000 | $1,400,000 | $1,860,000 |
| Miguel Black | General Counsel | $280,000 | $80,000 | $800,000 | $1,160,000 |

### Compensation Philosophy

Our compensation program is designed to attract and retain talented executives and align their interests with stockholder interests. We provide a balanced mix of base salary, performance-based bonuses, and equity awards. Base salary is set at market-competitive levels. Annual bonuses are based on achievement of financial and operational objectives. Equity awards vest over four years to encourage long-term value creation.

### Base Salary

Base salaries for our executive officers are established at levels we believe are competitive for similar positions at comparable companies. Salaries are reviewed annually and adjusted based on individual performance, scope of responsibilities, and market conditions.

### Annual Bonuses

Annual bonuses are paid based on achievement of company and individual objectives. In fiscal year 2030, bonuses were based on achievement of revenue targets, profitability targets, customer acquisition goals, and individual performance metrics. Bonuses for fiscal year 2030 were paid at 100% of target.

### Equity Awards

We grant equity awards in the form of stock options and restricted stock units (RSUs) to our executive officers. Equity awards are granted annually and vest over a four-year period with a one-year cliff. We believe equity awards align executive interests with stockholder interests and encourage long-term value creation.

### Executive Benefits

Our executive officers are eligible to participate in our health insurance, retirement, and other benefit plans on the same basis as other employees. We do not provide executive-specific perquisites or benefits.

### Change of Control Provisions

Our executive officers do not have change of control agreements. Equity awards include provisions allowing acceleration of vesting upon a change of control in certain circumstances.
